questions.question_text: Which deed provides the broadest protection for the buyer in Colorado?
questions.explanation: A general warranty deed provides the broadest protection — the grantor warrants title against all defects, including those that arose before the grantor owned the property.

questions.question_text: A special warranty deed protects the buyer against defects that arose:
distractors: At any time in the property's history
Only after the deed is recorded
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed limits the grantor's warranty to defects that arose during their period of ownership — not for the entire chain of title.

questions.question_text: A property owner wants to transfer their interest without making any guarantees about the title. They should use a:
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys only whatever interest the grantor may have with no warranties. It provides no protection to the grantee regarding title quality.

questions.question_text: A bargain and sale deed implies that the grantor:
questions.correct_answer: Holds title to convey but makes no express warranties
distractors: Guarantees title free of all defects
Makes no claim to owning the property
questions.explanation: A bargain and sale deed implies the grantor holds title and possession, but it contains no express warranties against encumbrances — less protection than a warranty deed, but a stronger implied claim than a quitclaim.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases property and later discovers a title defect from 30 years ago. Which deed would have protected the buyer?
questions.explanation: Only a general warranty deed protects against all title defects, including those from before the seller's ownership. A special warranty only covers the seller's period of ownership.

questions.question_text: Colorado is which type of recording state?
questions.explanation: Colorado is a race-notice state — the first person to record in good faith and for value prevails. Both good faith and recording priority matter.

questions.question_text: Documents affecting real property are recorded with the:
questions.explanation: Real property documents are recorded with the County Clerk and Recorder, providing constructive notice to the world of interests in the property.

questions.question_text: The Colorado documentary fee applies to:
questions.explanation: The documentary fee is charged on deeds and other documents that convey real property. The fee is based on the sale price and is paid at the time of recording.

questions.question_text: Recording a deed provides what type of notice?
questions.explanation: Recording provides constructive notice to the world — everyone is deemed to know about the recorded interest, whether or not they actually searched the records.

questions.question_text: Buyer A purchases a property on Monday but does not record. Buyer B purchases the same property on Tuesday and records immediately in good faith. Who has priority?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer B — first to record in good faith
distractors: The seller — they own it until resolved
questions.explanation: In a race-notice state like Colorado, the first to record in good faith and for value prevails. Buyer B recorded first and acted in good faith.

questions.question_text: Colorado's primary security instrument for real estate loans is the:
questions.explanation: Colorado uses deeds of trust, not mortgages, as the primary security instrument for real estate loans.

questions.question_text: In a Colorado deed of trust, the neutral third party who holds legal title is the:
questions.explanation: The public trustee is the neutral third party in a Colorado deed of trust. They hold bare legal title until the loan is satisfied.

questions.question_text: When a Colorado loan secured by a deed of trust is paid in full, the document recorded to clear the lien from title is a:
questions.explanation: The holder of the evidence of debt files a written request for release with the public trustee, and the public trustee — not the lender — executes and records the release of the deed of trust (C.R.S. 38-39-102). This replaces the 'satisfaction of mortgage' used in mortgage states.

questions.question_text: A homeowner sells their property without the lender's consent. The due-on-sale clause allows the lender to:
distractors: Sue the new buyer for all damages
questions.explanation: A due-on-sale clause allows the lender to accelerate the loan — demanding full payment of the remaining balance — if the property is transferred without consent.

questions.question_text: In a deed of trust, the borrower is called the:
questions.explanation: In Colorado's deed of trust, the borrower is the trustor (grantor), the lender is the beneficiary, and the public trustee is the neutral third party.

questions.question_text: The Colorado Housing and Finance Authority (CHFA) primarily assists:
questions.explanation: CHFA provides below-market-rate mortgage loans and down payment assistance primarily for first-time homebuyers, low-to-moderate income borrowers, and veterans.

questions.question_text: CHFA offers programs that include both first mortgage loans and:
questions.correct_answer: Down payment and closing cost help
questions.explanation: CHFA offers first mortgage loans and second mortgage assistance specifically for down payments and closing costs.

questions.question_text: A veteran looking for a state-sponsored mortgage program in Colorado should contact:
distractors: The Department of Veterans Affairs only
The state Division of Real Estate
questions.explanation: CHFA has programs specifically for veterans, in addition to programs for first-time homebuyers and low-to-moderate income borrowers.

questions.question_text: CHFA mortgage rates compared to conventional market rates are typically:
questions.explanation: CHFA — a self-funded public enterprise and political subdivision created by the legislature in 1973 (not a tax-funded state agency) — provides below-market-rate mortgage loans and down-payment assistance to qualifying borrowers.

questions.question_text: A seller offers to carry a second mortgage for the buyer. Under Colorado law, the broker must ensure:
distractors: The seller has a lending license
questions.explanation: Seller financing requires specific Commission-approved disclosure forms. The seller does not need a lending license, and the Commission does not pre-approve individual transactions.
